Use a third-party observability platform such as Grafana, Datadog, or New Relic — GCP's native observability tools are insufficient to provide adequate coverage for modern data platforms
BigQuery's INFORMATION_SCHEMA views (JOBS, TABLES, PARTITIONS) provide all the observability data needed across pipelines, storage, lineage, freshness, and data quality dimensions
explain: Comprehensive data platform observability requires layering: infrastructure metrics (Cloud Monitoring), log analytics (Cloud Logging), data quality (Dataplex), lineage (Dataplex Lineage API), freshness (custom metrics), and cost attribution (Cloud Billing labels). No single service covers all dimensions — they complement each other.
concept: Data platform observability, Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging, Dataplex, multi-layer monitoring

q: A data pipeline produces results that need to be approved by a business user before being published to a production table. How do you implement a human-in-the-loop approval step in Cloud Composer?
opts: Cloud Composer does not support any form of human approval step in a DAG — all DAG tasks must be fully automated and complete without external input from users

Use an Airflow ExternalTaskSensor that waits for a trigger from an external system, or use the Airflow API sensor that polls a BigQuery approval table. Business user approves in a UI that marks the record in BigQuery; sensor detects the approval and continues the DAG
Use Cloud Composer's built-in HumanApprovalOperator task type that natively pauses DAG execution and emails an approval link to the configured approver list and resumes on click
Pause the entire Cloud Composer environment via gcloud composer environments update during the manual approval window and resume it after the business user has approved
explain: Human-in-the-loop in Airflow: implement a custom sensor that polls an approval table in BigQuery or Firestore every N minutes. Business user reviews results in a dashboard and clicks Approve → writes a record to the approval table. The sensor detects the approval record and returns True, allowing downstream tasks to run.
concept: Airflow human-in-the-loop, custom sensors, approval workflows, ExternalTaskSensor

q: How do you ensure that sensitive BigQuery data is never written to Cloud Logging (Airflow logs may contain query results)?
opts: Sensitive BigQuery data never appears in Airflow logs because Airflow's logging framework only captures task lifecycle metadata and not the underlying SQL query result rows

Configure Airflow to minimize log verbosity (log_level=WARNING); avoid logging query results in BigQuery operators (set log_output=False); use Cloud Logging exclusions to filter out log entries from Composer tasks that may contain sensitive data; apply DLP scan on Cloud Logging to detect and redact PII in logs
Disable Cloud Logging entirely for the Cloud Composer environment by toggling off the integration in the environment configuration — this prevents any task logs from being persisted
Use VPC Service Controls perimeters around Cloud Logging to prevent log data containing sensitive results from being exported or accessed outside the perimeter boundary at all
explain: Preventing sensitive data in logs: (1) set Airflow log level to WARNING or ERROR — INFO logs often include SQL results; (2) configure BigQueryExecuteQueryOperator with log_output=False; (3) add Cloud Logging exclusion filters for specific Composer namespaces; (4) implement a Cloud Logging sink with DLP inline inspection to redact PII before logs are stored.
concept: Sensitive data in logs, Airflow log configuration, Cloud Logging DLP, log redaction

q: What is the recommended approach for managing secrets (API keys, database passwords) used by Cloud Composer DAGs?
opts: Store secrets directly in Airflow Variables via the Composer UI — they are stored encrypted in the Airflow metadata database and accessible to all DAGs via the Variable.get() API

Store secrets in Secret Manager; in Airflow DAGs, use the Secret Manager backend for Airflow Connections and Variables (configure airflow.cfg: [secrets] backend = airflow.providers.google.cloud.secrets.secret_manager.CloudSecretManagerBackend). Secrets are fetched from Secret Manager at runtime — never stored in the metadata DB or code
Hardcode secrets directly in DAG Python files committed to the source repository and uploaded to the Composer GCS bucket — access is restricted via IAM on the Composer bucket
Store secrets as environment variables on Dataproc clusters and access them from Airflow DAGs by submitting Spark jobs that read the environment and return values via XCom
explain: Cloud Composer + Secret Manager: configure Composer to use Secret Manager as the Airflow secrets backend. Airflow Connections and Variables prefixed with airflow-variables- and airflow-connections- in Secret Manager are automatically fetched at runtime. This keeps secrets out of the metadata DB, encrypted at rest, with full audit logging and rotation support.
concept: Secret Manager, Airflow secrets backend, Cloud Composer secret management

q: A data team needs to ensure that no Dataflow job in their organization can read from or write to resources outside of a specific GCP project list. How do you enforce this policy?
opts: Add VPC Firewall rules to every Dataflow worker VPC subnet that block egress traffic to any GCP API endpoints outside the approved list of project network ranges

Configure an Organization Policy constraint (gcp.resourceLocations or a custom OPA/Forseti policy) restricting Dataflow job network configuration to specific VPCs within approved projects; combine with VPC Service Controls perimeters that include only the approved projects
Manually review every Dataflow job configuration as part of a change advisory board approval gate before any pipeline is allowed to be deployed to production environments
Use Workload Identity Federation to restrict the Dataflow service account's token exchange so it can only impersonate identities scoped to the approved set of project IDs
explain: Organization Policy + VPC Service Controls for Dataflow resource restriction: VPC SC perimeters define which projects can communicate via GCP APIs. Dataflow workers within the perimeter can only access services also in the perimeter. Organization Policies (constraints/compute.restrictVpnPeerIPs, etc.) further restrict network access.
concept: Organization Policy, VPC Service Controls, Dataflow resource restrictions, enterprise governance

q: You need to monitor the health of a Pub/Sub subscription to ensure messages are being consumed in a timely manner. Which metric indicates consumption health?
opts: pubsub.googleapis.com/topic/send_message_operation_count — this counts published messages and reflects subscriber consumption health when compared against the publish baseline

pubsub.googleapis.com/subscription/num_undelivered_messages (backlog) and subscription/oldest_unacked_message_age — a growing backlog or increasing age indicates the subscriber is falling behind. Alert when oldest_unacked_message_age exceeds your processing SLA
pubsub.googleapis.com/subscription/pull_request_count — this counts subscriber pull API requests and indicates whether consumers are actively reading from the subscription
pubsub.googleapis.com/topic/byte_cost — total bytes consumed by all subscribers attached to the topic; rising bytes mean subscribers are keeping up with the publish rate
explain: Pub/Sub subscription health: num_undelivered_messages (backlog) shows unprocessed messages; oldest_unacked_message_age shows how old the oldest unprocessed message is. A growing backlog with increasing message age means the consumer is behind. Alert when message age > your max acceptable lag (e.g., 5 minutes).
concept: Pub/Sub subscription monitoring, backlog metrics, oldest_unacked_message_age, consumer health

q: A Dataflow pipeline needs to be tested before deployment. How do you run a subset of production data through the pipeline locally without incurring Dataflow costs?
opts: Dataflow jobs must always run in the managed cloud service — Apache Beam pipelines cannot be executed locally on developer machines or CI/CD agents without the DataflowRunner

Use the Apache Beam DirectRunner: run the pipeline locally with a small sample of data from Cloud Storage or BigQuery. The DirectRunner executes all Beam transforms locally on your machine (or CI/CD agent) without Dataflow infrastructure. Use pytest with the DirectRunner for automated unit and integration tests
Use Dataflow's simulation mode by passing --simulate_shuffle=true and --dry_run flags to the pipeline runner — this validates the pipeline graph without incurring full execution costs
Test only individual DoFn classes in isolation using standard Python or Java unit testing frameworks — full end-to-end pipeline testing on real data is unnecessary in practice
explain: DirectRunner for local testing: PipelineOptions.fromArgs(['--runner=DirectRunner']). The pipeline runs locally on JVM/Python interpreter. Use a sample BigQuery table (TABLESAMPLE or LIMIT 1000) or small Cloud Storage files as test input. DirectRunner supports all Beam features and produces identical logical results to DataflowRunner.
concept: Apache Beam DirectRunner, local pipeline testing, CI/CD pipeline validation

q: You need to implement automatic remediation when a Dataplex data quality check fails (e.g., rows with NULL customer_id should be moved to a quarantine table). How do you automate this?
opts: Manually review Dataplex scan results in the Cloud Console each time a scan fails and use bq query to move bad rows to the quarantine table after a manual investigation

Configure Dataplex Data Quality scan to write results to a BigQuery table; create a Cloud Monitoring alert on the Dataplex data quality metric; alert triggers a Pub/Sub notification → Cloud Function that queries the failed rows (using the scan result partition info) from the source table → inserts them into the quarantine table → optionally deletes from source
Dataplex automatically moves rows that fail any configured data quality rule to a system-managed quarantine table in the same dataset — no additional configuration is needed
Use a Cloud Composer DAG with a DataplexRunQualityScanOperator to schedule scans and an inline PythonOperator branch to handle the remediation logic after each scan completes
explain: Automated DQ remediation: Dataplex scan → writes results to BigQuery → Cloud Monitoring alert on failing rules → Pub/Sub → Cloud Function reads the scan result row filter (which rows failed which rules) → queries source BigQuery table for those rows → writes to quarantine_table → optionally DELETEs from source. Full automation from scan to remediation.
concept: Dataplex data quality remediation, automated quarantine, Cloud Monitoring alerts, Cloud Functions

q: A data team runs 500 BigQuery jobs per day. They want to identify the top 10 most expensive jobs by slot consumption in the last 7 days. Which query achieves this?
opts: Query the Cloud Billing export dataset in BigQuery, which includes a per-job cost breakdown column with job_id linkage for direct per-job cost ranking and analysis

SELECT job_id, total_slot_ms, total_bytes_processed, user_email FROM `project`.`region-us`.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS WHERE DATE(creation_time) >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL 7 DAY) AND state = 'DONE' ORDER BY total_slot_ms DESC LIMIT 10
Query Cloud Monitoring's BigQuery slot metrics filtered by job_id label to find the 10 jobs with the highest total slot consumption over a rolling 7-day window via MQL
Use the BigQuery Cloud Console's Recent Jobs view, sort the table by duration column descending, and visually pick the top 10 longest-running jobs from the past week
explain: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS contains per-job statistics: total_slot_ms (slot time = cost proxy for on-demand), total_bytes_processed, user_email, creation_time, job_id. Sort by total_slot_ms DESC LIMIT 10 gives the 10 most resource-intensive jobs. Use this for cost attribution, optimization targeting, and identifying runaway queries.
concept: BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS, job cost analysis, top expensive queries

q: How do you implement a rolling deployment for a Dataform project — deploying to 10% of users first, then 100% — in a BigQuery context?
opts: Dataform does not support partial deployment or progressive rollout patterns — every Dataform release tag is applied atomically to all downstream consumers at the same time

Use feature flags: maintain a user_cohort field; deploy Dataform models that JOIN to a feature_flags table to apply new logic only for cohort 1 (10% of users). After validation, expand the cohort to 100%. This is a SQL-level A/B deployment without infrastructure changes
Deploy the new Dataform release to a separate sibling BigQuery project containing the data of the canary 10% of users, and route those users' queries to the new project via DNS
Use BigQuery TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(10 PERCENT) in the consumer view to randomly route exactly 10% of incoming queries to the newly deployed Dataform model output table for canary
explain: SQL-level rolling deployment: add a user_cohort or ab_test_flag to your user dimension. Deploy new Dataform models with: CASE WHEN user_cohort = 'experiment' THEN new_logic ELSE old_logic END. Only users in the experiment cohort see new results. Gradually expand cohort size from 10% to 100% via a config table update — no new deployment needed.
concept: Dataform rolling deployment, A/B testing in SQL, feature flags, progressive rollout
